questions.q: What are the three pillars of observability?

questions.a: A metric that only increases or resets.
A metric that can go up and down.

questions.q: What is a histogram used for?
questions.a: Measuring the distribution of values such as latency.
A service level indicator, the measurement that reflects user experience.

questions.q: What is a trace ID for?
questions.a: Correlating all logs and spans for one request or workflow.
Too many unique label values make metrics expensive and hard to query.

questions.q: Why do structured logs help incidents?
questions.a: They make it easier to filter, search, and correlate events.
They detect when an error budget is being consumed too quickly.

questions.q: Why is sampling used in tracing?
questions.a: To reduce telemetry volume while still capturing representative traces.

questions.q: Why do tail latencies matter in SLOs?
questions.a: Rare slow requests often determine whether users perceive the service as healthy.

questions.q: Why do logs and traces complement each other?
questions.a: Traces show request flow, while logs capture detailed context and errors.

questions.q: When should you use traces instead of logs?
questions.a: When you need request-level causality across multiple services.

questions.q: When is high-cardinality data a bad metric label?
questions.a: When each request or user creates a near-unique label value.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with aggressive alerting?
questions.a: You catch more issues, but alert fatigue and noise increase.

questions.q: Why should an SLO be tied to user impact?
questions.a: Because telemetry should measure what the user actually experiences.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with very high trace sampling?
questions.a: You gain more coverage, but storage and ingestion costs rise sharply.

questions.q: Why does observability improve debugging more than raw data volume?
questions.a: Because well-shaped signals reduce search time better than noisy logs do.
